calendar.matters:
MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE
Off Calendar Sep-25-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

RESPONDENTs ALLIANCE RESIDENTIAL, LLC,and ROBERT HALL's MOTION TO ADMIT COUNSEL PRO HAC VICE.

Respondents Alliance Residential, LLC and Robert Michael Hall's unopposed Motion to Admit Counsel Pro Hac Vice is GRANTED.

Good cause appearing, counsel Amy E. Furness may appear as counsel pro hac vice for Moving Parties in this action.

Counsel for the moving party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the court's ruling and must drop a hard courtesy copy in Dept. 302's inbox.
Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/JMQ)

Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Quash Foreign Subpoenas To Nonparties Samsara Inc. And Dean Cusumano Or, In The Altenrative, For A Protective Order
Off Calendar Sep-09-2025 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: PETITIONER SAMSARA INC.'S PETITION TO QUASH FOREIGN SUBPOENAS TO NONPARTIES SAMSARA INC. AND DEAN CUSUMANO OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, FOR A PROTECTIVE ORDER

Appearances: Benjamin Halbig and Alex Ramsey of Jenner & Block (628.267.6834) for petitioner and moving party; Spencer Kelly of Lewis Brisbois Bisgaard & Smith LLP (415.362.2580) appearing with Amy Furness of Carlton Fields (pro hac vice pending) (305.539.7253) for respondents and opposing parties.

Counsel appear to contest the court's tentative ruling as issued below:

Petitioner Samsara Inc.'s motion to quash foreign subpoenas or in the alternative, for a protective order is granted in part and denied in part.

"As between parties to litigation and nonparties, the burden of discovery should be placed on the latter only if the former do not possess the material sought to be discovered. An exception to this may exist where a showing is made the material obtained from the party is unreliable and may be subject to impeachment by material in possession of the nonparty." (Calcor Space Facility, Inc. v. Superior Court (1997) 53 Cal.App.4th 216, 225.)

Here, respondents served PMQ subpoenas and a subpoena regarding former employee Cusumano on nonparty petitioner.

The PMQ subpoenas call for petitioner to prepare a corporate representative to testify on seven categories that seek discovery into aspects of petitioner's dashcam and dashboard menu (Topics 1, 3), the specific features and functions of CWC's dashcam and dashboard (Topics 2, 4, and 5), CWC's "use" of Samsara's "platform" (Topic 6), and petitioner's data retention policy (Topic 7).

The court finds good cause to allow the PMQ deposition as to Topics 2, 4, 5, 6 and 7. These requests are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) The record shows that CWC needed help in responding to respondent's discovery requests regarding petitioner's dashboard. (Furness Decl., par. 12, 16; Halbig Decl., par. 9.) CWC also failed to turn over all responsive information (incident report and spreadsheet) to respondent. (Furness Decl., par. 8.) The court reasonably infers that CWC's may have been unreliable or incomplete.

The court finds good cause to disallow the PMQ deposition as to Topics 1 and 3. Respondent wants Petitioner to testify on wildly overbroad matters of the entirety of the technical architecture, features and operations of the Samsara CM31 and the dashboard menu available to any Samsara customer, apparently without regard to product or any connection to CWC. Respondents showing of relevance is modest. Petitioner's showing of burden is substantial. The relevance is clearly outweighed by the burden and the court prohibits inquiry into topics 1 and 3. Surely, Respondent could pick up a user manual to get much, if not all of this, information. On this record, the burden cannot be on the developer, manufacturer or seller to provide such instruction through deposition.

Respondent fails to demonstrate good cause to depose former employee Cusumano. He is a former employee and from what the court can discern, merely attended one Zoom meeting involving CWC's disclosures.

The request for sanctions is denied. Both sides acted with substantial justification.

To the extent petitioner seeks additional or different relief, the motion is denied.

The matter is argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the court takes the motion under submission. Judge: Joseph M. Quinn, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Reporter: Carol Haraburda, CSR 8052 (T: 925.360.6065, E: chreporter@comcast.net)
